facts.content: The WHO Global Report on Traditional and Complementary Medicine (2019) lists acupuncture, herbal medicines, indigenous traditional medicine, homeopathy, traditional Chinese medicine, naturopathy, chiropractic, osteopathy, ayurvedic medicine, and Unani medicine as systems of traditional medicine used globally.

Albert Abrams believed that drugs functioned by possessing the same or similar vibrations as the diseases they were intended to cure, a concept he considered analogous to the teachings of Samuel Hahnemann, the founder of homeopathy.

Samuel Hahnemann developed homeopathy in the late eighteenth century based on the principle of "like cures like," which posits that a substance producing symptoms in a healthy person can treat similar symptoms in a sick person when prepared in extreme dilution.

26 percent of physicians believed in the efficacy of homeopathy, and 23 percent of physicians believed in the efficacy of herbal approaches.

Homeopathy was developed in the late 18th century by the German physician Samuel Hahnemann as a reaction to the harsh medical treatments of that time.
The overall scientific consensus regarding homeopathy is skeptical of its efficacy, despite reports of positive experiences from some individuals and practitioners.
Homeopathy is neither effective nor rational, despite its continued persistence, according to the authors of the article "Homeopathy—where is the science? A current inventory on a ...".

Ernst (2002) notes that the foundational concepts and claimed mechanisms of action in homeopathy often conflict with established principles of chemistry, biology, and pharmacology, leading to debate between proponents and skeptics regarding empirical evidence and biological plausibility.
Observational studies and real-world evidence offer insights into the effectiveness of homeopathy that extend beyond the findings of controlled trials.
Observational studies in homeopathy lack the control of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) but are considered to better reflect real-world usage and patient experiences.

The broader scientific community remains skeptical of homeopathy due to a lack of consistent reproducibility and mechanistic clarity, despite some evidence supporting its clinical efficacy in individualized and holistic care contexts.
Homeopathy operates within a vitalistic and holistic philosophical paradigm, whereas conventional biomedicine is grounded in empirical reductionism, materialism, and the scientific method.
Homeopathy posits that healing arises from the stimulation of a 'vital force'—defined as an immaterial life energy—rather than through the direct biochemical alteration of physiological processes.

The study measured patient and physician assessments on numeric rating scales (0-10) and quality of life at baseline, 3, 12, and 24 months, concluding that quality of life and disease severity showed significant improvement after two years for conditions including allergic rhinitis in men, headache in women, and atopic dermatitis in children.

A 6-year observational study by Thompson, E.A. et al., published in the Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine in 2005, followed 6,544 patients treated homeopathically. The study reported that 70% of patients experienced improved health, with 50% showing major improvement in conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease, irritable bowel syndrome, menopausal problems, and migraines, while 80% of children showed improvement and 65% showed major improvements in eczema and asthma.

In an 8-year observational study published in BMC Public Health in 2008, Witt, CM et al. studied 3,709 patients, with 73% contributing data to the 8-year follow-up. The study concluded that patients seeking homeopathic treatment for conditions such as allergic rhinitis, headache, atopic dermatitis, and multiple recurrent infections are likely to improve considerably, with effects persisting for as long as 8 years.

In the 2001 study of 800 patients treated homeopathically, 89% of patients reported that their health had improved after homeopathic treatment, compared to 13% who reported improvement after regular treatment.

Following homeopathic treatment, the number of visits patients paid to regular medical specialists decreased, and more than half of the treated patients were able to reduce their medicine use significantly.

Extrapolating the cost-saving effects of reduced medicine use and fewer specialist visits observed in the 2001 study of homeopathic patients, the Belgian state could have saved €775 million per annum.
